title: Chest X-Ray Screening in Ugandan Refugee Settlements

identified_risks.explanation: The AI model was validated on data from fixed health facilities, but the portable device produces images under different lighting, resolution, and patient positioning conditions in the refugee settlement, increasing the likelihood of misclassification.
When offline, the system queues results locally and syncs only after 48 to 72 hours, meaning that positive findings are not immediately available for clinical action.
Patients who screen positive are advised to return for follow-up, yet the mobile nature of the settlement’s population makes it difficult to locate them, risking untreated tuberculosis cases.

Results are stored locally while the device is offline; without explicit mention of encryption or secure storage, sensitive patient data could be exposed if the device is lost or accessed by unauthorized individuals.
The scenario does not describe obtaining explicit consent from refugees before screening, raising concerns about respecting autonomy in a vulnerable population.
The training data from fixed facilities may not reflect the genetic, environmental, or socioeconomic characteristics of the refugee population, potentially leading to systematic misdiagnosis.
The device relies on solar power with intermittent availability; power outages could interrupt scanning or data capture, reducing overall screening coverage.
There is no indication that local health authorities approved or monitored the deployment, which could result in non-compliance with national health regulations.
The AI model has not been evaluated on images produced by the portable device under field conditions, so its performance in the actual deployment environment remains unknown.
Inaccurate positives could lead to unnecessary follow‑up visits and strain limited medical resources in the settlement, potentially diverting care from truly sick individuals.

overall_assessment: The deployment faces high risks from potential misclassification due to domain shift and from the inability to reliably follow up with patients who test positive, both of which threaten timely treatment and could worsen disease spread. Delayed result synchronization and lack of local regulatory oversight further compound these safety concerns.

Resource-Constrained Deployment.explanation: The device relies on solar power with intermittent availability; power outages could interrupt scanning or data capture, reducing overall screening coverage.

Distribution Shift and Local Validation.explanation: The AI model has not been evaluated on images produced by the portable device under field conditions, so its performance in the actual deployment environment remains unknown.

Monitoring and Incident Reporting.explanation: Patients who screen positive are advised to return for follow-up, yet the mobile nature of the settlement’s population makes it difficult to locate them, risking untreated tuberculosis cases.

scenario_summary.title: Chest X-Ray Screening in Ugandan Refugee Settlements

limitations_and_dual_use_considerations: Expected risks are researcher-defined and should be reviewed by domain experts.
Scoring uses automated keyword matching with a specificity requirement and should be audited by humans.
The benchmark could be used to fine-tune models to game evaluation; this is mitigated by requiring scenario-specific explanations.

benchmark_explanation: Delayed results in a mobile population undermine effective follow-up. Portable device image quality may differ from validation conditions. No mechanism tracks whether positive cases receive follow-up care.
